evidence: R1 routing: has route for 172.16.0.0/24 via next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R1 routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list entry: {"prefix": "172.16.0.0", "fwd-list": [{"fwd": "10.0.0.2"}]}).

R2 routing: no specific route for 192.168.0.0/24 — routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list contains only a default route ({"prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [{"fwd": "10.0.0.6"}]}) and no entry for 192.168.0.0/24.

missing_info: Provide R2's full active routing table (show ip route) to confirm absence of 192.168.0.0/24 and to see current next-hops.
Confirm that host 172.16.0.12 is up and has correct default gateway (172.16.0.1) / provide R2's ARP table entry for 172.16.0.12.

plan_history.intent_description: R2 has no route to 192.168.0.0/24, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 cannot reach 192.168.0.12. Restore bidirectional reachability by adding a return route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 pointing to R1.
plan_history.problem: R2 missing route for 192.168.0.0/24; return traffic from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12 is unroutable.
plan_history.fix_summary: Add a static route on router2 for 192.168.0.0/24 via next-hop 10.0.0.1 (R1) using Serial0/1/0.

remaining_issues: Access-list R2_TO_R1 on R2 includes an explicit deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443; this may block HTTPS traffic between the LANs and should be validated as intentional.
End-to-end host verification (e.g., ping/traceroute or an application test from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12) has not been shown — recommend one host-to-host test to confirm full connectivity.
